old_value: {"id":"bs-cuk","title":"Define strategy as a concept","description":"Define what a strategy is within bjornstack. This is the foundational task — all other workflow decisions depend on it. Must produce: (1) Document how the four layers relate through the WHY/HOW/WHAT frame: Principle=WHY (why it matters), Strategy=HOW (the abstract approach), Guideline=WHAT (concrete language-specific steps), Guard=enforcement of WHAT. This framing should be part of README.md and any format docs. (2) A format spec for strategy files (like FORMAT.md for principles). What fields? What level of detail? What makes a good vs bad strategy? (3) Clear definition of the boundary between strategy and guideline — when does something belong in the strategy vs in a language-specific guideline? (4) When is a strategy needed vs when a principle is direct enough to skip straight to guidelines? (5) Define the divergence documentation format — how to record when a language can't follow part of a strategy. (6) Update README.md to reflect the four-layer system with WHY/HOW/WHAT framing. (7) Create a FORMAT equivalent for strategies.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-01T11:30:32Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-01T11:30:32Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"Create a new skill at .claude/skills/strategy-editor/SKILL.md. Three modes (mirroring principle-editor): Create, Review, Extract.

Create mode: (1) User describes a technique or provides a principle name. (2) Draft the strategy in strategies/FORMAT.md format. (3) Run quality checks: name is 2-4 word noun phrase, PRINCIPLE link references a real principle, steps are numbered/abstract/language-agnostic, OUTCOMES are observable, MISAPPLICATION is realistic, SKIP WHEN is present. (4) Layer check: not a principle (needs multiple steps), not a guideline (no language references), not a tutorial. (5) Coherence check: steps follow from principle, outcomes satisfy the principle, misapplication is realistic, skip-when is honest. (6) Write to strategies/<kebab-name>.md. (7) Offer handoff to guidelines-editor.

Review mode: Evaluate a draft strategy against all quality checks. Show concrete rewrites for failures.

Extract mode: Given prose docs or best practices, identify strategies vs. other layers. Produce formatted strategies.

Reference: docs/strategy-creation-workflow.md for full workflow context."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-p0b","title":"Create strategy-editor skill","description":"Create a new skill at .claude/skills/strategy-editor/SKILL.md. The skill: (1) Reads the source principle. (2) Defines the abstract, language-agnostic approach for implementing the principle. (3) Runs quality checks against the strategy format spec (from Task 1). (4) Writes to implementation/strategies/\u003cname\u003e.md. (5) Should identify which parts of the strategy are likely to have language-specific divergences (e.g., patterns that depend on inheritance, generics, or other language features).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-01T11:30:56Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-01T11:30:56Z"}

{"id":"bs-ftj","title":"Enhance principle-editor skill","description":"Add an 'After approval' section to .claude/skills/principle-editor/SKILL.md. Steps after user approves: (1) Uniqueness check — glob principles/*.md (excluding FORMAT.md), compare names and definitions for collision or semantic overlap. (2) Write principle to principles/\u003ckebab-name\u003e.md. No beads created — the audit detects that a strategy and guidelines are needed.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-01T11:30:52Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-01T11:30:52Z"}

(3) How the skills drive the pipeline. (4) The audit command — what it checks (missing strategies, missing guidelines, stale guidelines, undocumented divergences), how to run it. (5) The canonical list of supported languages. (6) How to add a new supported language. (7) Guard candidate identification and tracking. (8) Judgment-only and not-applicable handling.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-01T11:30:48Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-01T11:30:48Z"}

old_value: {"id":"bs-k0x","title":"Create guidelines-editor skill","description":"Create a new skill at .claude/skills/guidelines-editor/SKILL.md. The skill: (1) Reads the source principle and its strategy. Refuses to proceed if no strategy exists — prompts user to create one first. (2) Targets a specific language. (3) Walks through the strategy point by point, deriving concrete language-specific guidelines. (4) For each strategy point, checks: can this be followed as-is in this language? If not, documents the divergence and why in implementation/\u003clang\u003e/\u003cname\u003e/divergences.md. (5) For each guideline, assesses guard candidacy ('Can this be mechanically checked with zero judgment?'). (6) Writes guidelines to implementation/\u003clang\u003e/\u003cname\u003e/guidelines.md, including a 'Guard candidates' section. (7) Creates a bead for each guard candidate. (8) For judgment-only or not-applicable principles, writes a file explaining why.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-01T11:31:01Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-01T11:31:01Z"}

{"id":"bs-gjj","title":"Review principle-editor against strategy-editor patterns","description":"Building the strategy-editor surfaced several patterns the principle-editor predates and doesn't have. Review and apply:

Structural gaps:
1. No annotated example — add one showing why each part of a real principle works
2. No explicit 'wait for approval' step in Create mode — agent could write to disk without pausing
3. No Update mode — principles evolve, need a mode for revising existing ones
4. No Review mode output structure — should lead with summary, then failures with rewrites
5. Extract mode doesn't create beads for other layers — should route cross-layer handoffs through beads

Consistency gaps:
6. No shaping step for raw input — users arrive with vague rules or frustrations, need help forming a one-sentence principle before drafting
7. No density guidance — when to use 1 vs 2 violations, how long WHY should be

Reference: .claude/skills/strategy-editor/SKILL.md for the patterns to mirror.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-01T13:59:38Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-01T13:59:38Z"}

new_value: All 7 gaps addressed: annotated example, wait-for-approval, Update mode, Review output structure, Extract bead routing, raw input shaping, density guidance

{"description":"Create strategy for No Shared Fate. Covers: factory-based test setup (each test gets fresh scoped state), per-test isolation for parallel safety, no shared package-level mutable state in tests, tests that work in imperfect conditions (not requiring pristine DB). Also covers: defaults that match production — test helpers should default to production-realistic configuration, not convenient fictions (e.g., in-memory store when prod uses PostgreSQL). Divergent defaults are a FEAL concern that manifests in test setup. Go guidelines to define underneath: per-test factory pattern, t.Parallel() usage, scoped test identities (unique user/team/document per test), no package-level var in test files holding mutable state. Cross-references: related to 'Compliance Test Suites' (uses factory pattern)."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-033","title":"Create strategy: Isolated Test Execution","description":"Create strategy for No Shared Fate. Covers: factory-based test setup (each test gets fresh state), per-test isolation for parallel safety, no shared package-level mutable state in tests, tests that don't require unrealistic pristine conditions. Go guidelines to define underneath: per-test factory pattern, t.Parallel() usage, no package-level var in test files holding mutable state. Cross-references: related to 'Compliance Test Suites' (uses factory pattern).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:18Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:18Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"Epic to validate whether the principle/strategy/guidelines/guard layers each add meaningful value. Test task: build a Fastmail JMAP email-to-Atom-feed service.

## Test Task Description

Build a service that:
1. Connects to Fastmail via JMAP
2. Lists all mailboxes, displays them nested with checkboxes in <ol> elements
3. Saves checkbox state; on first save for a folder, fetches all unread emails
4. Creates folder structure in output dir mirroring selected mailboxes
5. Creates initial-archive.xml (Atom feed with all unread) and feed.xml (empty, with prev-archive pointing to initial-archive.xml)

### Features
- **refresh**: Opens selected folders, finds new emails since last check, appends to feed.xml
- **prune**: Moves items older than 1 week from feed.xml into archive-<ISO year>w<ISO week>.xml. New archive gets prev-archive pointing to feed.xml current prev-archive. feed.xml prev-archive updates to point to the new archive file. feed.xml always points to the current week.

### Testing Requirements
- E2E tests with Playwright that verify the full flow works
- E2E tests MUST NOT hit Fastmail — use captured/mocked JMAP responses (do not prescribe a specific mocking tool; let the agent choose its approach)
- Unit tests for the core logic (feed construction, archive chain management, etc.)

### Success Criteria
- Well-tested, working code
- Follows design and testing principles (where applicable per test condition)
- Minimal handholding needed

## Experiment Design
Run the test task under different conditions and compare:
- How much intervention/correction was needed
- Quality of architecture and abstractions
- Test quality (behavior-driven vs coverage theater)
- Correctness of Atom feed structure and archive chain logic
- What testing approach the agent chose for API mocking (signal for design judgment)
- Overall code quality"}

old_value: {"id":"bs-6tr","title":"Create strategy: Minimal Public Surface","description":"Addresses feedback item 6: no principle covers what crosses the API surface between packages. SII says where to put things, CoC says what code does, but neither addresses what gets exported. A package's public surface is its contract — everything exported is a promise. May be a strategy under SII+BYN+CoC or may earn principle status ('Boundaries Are Contracts'). Explore during authoring. Related: bs-u06 (DFP), bs-y2f (TVS).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-11T15:26:05Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-11T15:26:05Z"}

new_value: Strategy written at strategies/intentionally-public.md. 6 steps covering default-unexported through audit. Rationale covers cross-language test pressure and coherence requirement.

{"description":"Create strategy for Highlight the Difference and Fail Early and Loud. Covers: table-driven test layout for 3+ similar cases, strategy function pattern (replace if/else with function-typed struct fields), all fields explicit in every test case, uniform test body executing same code path for every case. Also covers scenario naming (folded from bs-691): BDD-style naming where test names describe situation and expected outcome, names that read as sentences and serve as living specifications, long names preferred over vague short names — this concern wasn't strong enough to stand alone as a strategy but is important and should be addressed in this strategy's naming guidance. Go guidelines to define underneath: table test struct conventions, strategy function signatures, no optional struct fields, individual tests for 1-2 cases, Test\u003cType\u003e_\u003cMethod\u003e outer function naming, subtest/table case names as BDD scenarios, 'expected X to Y' assertion message phrasing. Guard candidates: lint for if/switch in test body functions. Cross-references: related to 'Test Data Builders' and 'Whole-Object Assertion'."}

Cross-references: related to 'Test Data Builders' and 'Whole-Object Assertion'.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:15Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:15Z"}

new_value: Folded into bs-7km (Parameterized Test Structure) — scenario naming is a concern within that strategy, not standalone

{"description":"Strategy for Structure Is Intent + Be Your Name. Addresses package organization: naming, type placement, dependency direction, library wrapping. Absorbs Boundary Type Placement. Also covers domain-driven package structure (folded from bs-4tw): organizing code by domain (cart/, pricing/, notifications/) not by technical role (models/, controllers/, services/), package boundaries that constrain where code belongs, eliminating catch-all packages. Also covers namespace-aware naming (folded from bs-n5q): smurf naming avoidance (mail.Mail, user.User) where the exported name stutters against the package name — in Go and Python where the package is always part of the qualified name, the export name should add information not repeat it. These concerns weren't strong enough to stand alone but are important and should be addressed in this strategy. Go guidelines to define underneath: package naming conventions, no utils/helpers/common packages, internal/ usage patterns, smurf-naming avoidance rules. Guard candidates: lint for banned package names (utils, helpers, common, misc), lint for exported type names that match package name."}

new_value: {"notes":"FOUNDATION STRATEGY: downstream strategies reference the boundary validation pattern defined here. Higher coherence bar — vocabulary established here propagates everywhere. When writing this strategy, reference: Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for how validation relates to the compute/coordinate split (validation is computation at the boundary, called by a coordinator). Domain-First Packaging (bs-u06) for where boundaries live in the package structure. Grow Don't Break (bs-uzp) for the tolerant reader principle (accept unknown additions, reject unknown values you branch on)."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-61m","title":"Create strategy: Scrub on Entry","description":"Strategy for Fail Early and Loud. Validate everything at system boundaries before it enters domain logic. The refined Postel's Law: be tolerant of additions you don't use (unknown fields are safe to ignore — this lets upstream systems grow per Grow Don't Break), strict about values you depend on (an enum you don't recognize in a field you act on is unsafe). If your code branches on it, computes with it, or stores it as a fact, validate it. DDD anti-corruption layer concept: the system interior works only with pristine objects that have passed all checks. Uncle Bob's clean architecture boundary: tight core, validated inputs. The biological metaphor: like surgical scrubbing, you clean at the boundary to protect what's inside.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-12T11:01:09Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:01:09Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"When writing this strategy, reference: Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for the boolean-mode → strategy-function refactoring (coordinators vs computation informs when an abstraction is mixing concerns). Test Data Builders (bs-rxe) as a concrete example of earned abstraction (extract builder after 3+ tests need the same setup)."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-5ht","title":"Create strategy: Earned Abstraction","description":"Create strategy for Earned Abstraction. Covers: the rule of three (don't abstract until 3 concrete cases), recognizing the boolean-mode smell as a signal of premature abstraction, refactoring mode-flag abstractions into strategy pattern (named collaborator + shared core/harness), when duplication is preferable to abstraction. Go guidelines to define underneath: function types as strategy collaborators, extracting shared core with varying strategy, recognizing boolean/mode parameters as a smell. Guard candidates: lint for boolean parameters that switch behavior in otherwise shared functions. Cross-references: upstream of 'Coordinator Test Design' (CoC), connects to Consistent Beats Correct (keep duplication over wrong abstraction).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:51:41Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:51:41Z"}

{"notes":"When writing this strategy, reference: Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for production-realistic defaults vs convenient fictions (test defaults that diverge from prod are a FEAL concern). Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for how isolation requirements differ between compute tests (stateless, naturally isolated) and coordinator tests (mock lifecycle)."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-033","title":"Create strategy: Isolated Test Execution","description":"Create strategy for No Shared Fate. Covers: factory-based test setup (each test gets fresh scoped state), per-test isolation for parallel safety, no shared package-level mutable state in tests, tests that work in imperfect conditions (not requiring pristine DB). Also covers: defaults that match production — test helpers should default to production-realistic configuration, not convenient fictions (e.g., in-memory store when prod uses PostgreSQL). Divergent defaults are a FEAL concern that manifests in test setup. Go guidelines to define underneath: per-test factory pattern, t.Parallel() usage, scoped test identities (unique user/team/document per test), no package-level var in test files holding mutable state. Cross-references: related to 'Compliance Test Suites' (uses factory pattern).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:18Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:27:05Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"When writing this strategy, reference: Domain-First Packaging (bs-u06) for where service boundaries map to package structure. Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for how services validate incoming data at boundaries. Grow Don't Break (bs-uzp) for how service APIs evolve without breaking consumers."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-v9e","title":"Create strategy: Service Boundary Communication","description":"Create strategy for No Shared Fate and Structure Is Intent. Covers: services communicate through APIs or messages, not shared databases. Each service owns its data and exposes it through explicit interfaces. No reaching into another service's tables. Architecture-level — guidelines are per-stack. Hard line: if two services need the same data, one owns it and the other requests it.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:20Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:20Z"}

new_value: Folded into bs-4tw (Domain-First Packaging) — domain-driven package structure is the same concern from a different angle

{"notes":"When writing this strategy, reference: Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for the tolerant reader pattern (be tolerant of additions, strict on values you branch on — this is the consumer-side of GDB). Service Boundary Communication (bs-v9e) for what contracts exist at boundaries. Flag Your Changes (bs-jgv) for how feature flags serve as the mechanism when breaking changes are genuinely necessary."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-uzp","title":"Create strategy: Grow Don't Break","description":"Strategy for No Shared Fate. Only additive changes to public/external APIs — new endpoints, new fields, new capabilities. Never remove, rename, or go back on a previously stated fact. When breaking changes are genuinely necessary, use semantic versioning (major = breaking) so consumers opt in explicitly. Help consumers upgrade when a major version bump is needed. Rich Hickey's accretion vs breakage framing: a system that grows is one others can depend on; a system that breaks teaches consumers to fear upgrades. Note distinction from Intentionally Public strategy (SII): GDB governs external API contracts at service boundaries; IP governs code-level export decisions within a package.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-12T11:01:05Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:01:05Z"}

new_value: {"priority":2}
old_value: {"id":"bs-owr","title":"Create strategy: Coordinator Test Design","description":"Create strategy for Compute or Coordinate and Consistent Beats Correct. Covers: classifying code as computation vs coordination, mocking all direct collaborators (not transitive), ~2N test count heuristic, coordinator smells (if-beyond-error, hidden collaborators, global deps). Go guidelines to define underneath: mock all-or-none implementation, function types for single-method deps, mock.Test(t), max 3-4 collaborators heuristic. Cross-references: related to strategy 'Public API Surface Testing' (CoC overlap).","notes":"FOUNDATION STRATEGY: downstream strategies reference the compute/coordinate classification defined here. Higher coherence bar — vocabulary established here propagates everywhere. When writing this strategy, reference: Domain-First Packaging (bs-u06) for where domain types live between compute and coordinate modules. Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for how boundary validation interacts with coordinator error handling.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:08Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:50:10Z"}

new_value: {"priority":2}
old_value: {"id":"bs-u06","title":"Create strategy: Domain-First Packaging","description":"Strategy for Structure Is Intent + Be Your Name. Addresses package organization: naming, type placement, dependency direction, library wrapping. Absorbs Boundary Type Placement. Also covers domain-driven package structure (folded from bs-4tw): organizing code by domain (cart/, pricing/, notifications/) not by technical role (models/, controllers/, services/), package boundaries that constrain where code belongs, eliminating catch-all packages. Also covers namespace-aware naming (folded from bs-n5q): smurf naming avoidance (mail.Mail, user.User) where the exported name stutters against the package name — in Go and Python where the package is always part of the qualified name, the export name should add information not repeat it. These concerns weren't strong enough to stand alone but are important and should be addressed in this strategy. Go guidelines to define underneath: package naming conventions, no utils/helpers/common packages, internal/ usage patterns, smurf-naming avoidance rules. Guard candidates: lint for banned package names (utils, helpers, common, misc), lint for exported type names that match package name.","notes":"FOUNDATION STRATEGY: downstream strategies reference the packaging decisions defined here. Higher coherence bar — vocabulary established here propagates everywhere. When writing this strategy, reference: Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for how compute/coordinate classification maps to package boundaries. Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for validation at package/service boundaries. Intentionally Public (existing strategy) for export decisions within the packaging model.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-11T15:25:58Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:50:11Z"}

new_value: {"priority":2}
old_value: {"id":"bs-61m","title":"Create strategy: Scrub on Entry","description":"Strategy for Fail Early and Loud. Validate everything at system boundaries before it enters domain logic. The refined Postel's Law: be tolerant of additions you don't use (unknown fields are safe to ignore — this lets upstream systems grow per Grow Don't Break), strict about values you depend on (an enum you don't recognize in a field you act on is unsafe). If your code branches on it, computes with it, or stores it as a fact, validate it. DDD anti-corruption layer concept: the system interior works only with pristine objects that have passed all checks. Uncle Bob's clean architecture boundary: tight core, validated inputs. The biological metaphor: like surgical scrubbing, you clean at the boundary to protect what's inside.","notes":"FOUNDATION STRATEGY: downstream strategies reference the boundary validation pattern defined here. Higher coherence bar — vocabulary established here propagates everywhere. When writing this strategy, reference: Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for how validation relates to the compute/coordinate split (validation is computation at the boundary, called by a coordinator). Domain-First Packaging (bs-u06) for where boundaries live in the package structure. Grow Don't Break (bs-uzp) for the tolerant reader principle (accept unknown additions, reject unknown values you branch on).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-12T11:01:09Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:50:12Z"}

Earned Abstraction (bs-5ht) for when to extract test helpers into builders (rule of three). Whole-Object Assertion (bs-8bd) for how builders create expected values for comparison."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-rxe","title":"Create strategy: Test Data Builders","description":"Create strategy for Highlight the Difference and Fail Early and Loud. Covers: a.* shared builders for domain objects used across test files, local options pattern for SUT configuration, when to extract from options to builders, concern methods (IsValid, IsSaved, IsNotSaved), With* field modifiers, Modify() escape hatch. Go guidelines to define underneath: a.* builder conventions in test/a/ package, options pattern for SUT, Build() returns value not pointer, static UUIDs for reproducibility. Cross-references: related to 'Whole-Object Assertion' and 'Parameterized Test Structure'.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:12Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:12Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"When writing this strategy, reference: Test Data Builders (bs-rxe) for how builders populate table test cases. Whole-Object Assertion (bs-8bd) for assertion patterns within table tests. Note: this strategy also covers scenario naming (folded from bs-691)."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-7km","title":"Create strategy: Parameterized Test Structure","description":"Create strategy for Highlight the Difference and Fail Early and Loud. Covers: table-driven test layout for 3+ similar cases, strategy function pattern (replace if/else with function-typed struct fields), all fields explicit in every test case, uniform test body executing same code path for every case. Also covers scenario naming (folded from bs-691): BDD-style naming where test names describe situation and expected outcome, names that read as sentences and serve as living specifications, long names preferred over vague short names — this concern wasn't strong enough to stand alone as a strategy but is important and should be addressed in this strategy's naming guidance. Go guidelines to define underneath: table test struct conventions, strategy function signatures, no optional struct fields, individual tests for 1-2 cases, Test\u003cType\u003e_\u003cMethod\u003e outer function naming, subtest/table case names as BDD scenarios, 'expected X to Y' assertion message phrasing. Guard candidates: lint for if/switch in test body functions. Cross-references: related to 'Test Data Builders' and 'Whole-Object Assertion'.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:15Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:49:58Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"When writing this strategy, reference: Test Data Builders (bs-rxe) for builders that create expected values. Parameterized Test Structure (bs-7km) for how whole-object assertion works within table-driven tests."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-8bd","title":"Create strategy: Whole-Object Assertion","description":"Create strategy for Fail Early and Loud and Highlight the Difference. Covers: comparing complete objects instead of cherry-picking fields, injecting deterministic sources for timestamps/UUIDs, using builders for expected values. Go guidelines to define underneath: require.* over assert.*, non-deterministic field handling patterns (inject clocks, UUID generators, or zero-and-compare). Guard candidates: lint for \u003eN field-by-field assertions when a builder exists for the type. Cross-references: tightly related to strategies 'Test Data Builders' and 'Parameterized Test Structure'.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:10Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-10T17:07:10Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"Largely standalone. When writing this strategy, reference: Flag Your Changes (bs-jgv) for how flags enable the tidy-then-change workflow by letting you ship structural changes behind an inert flag."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-p73","title":"Create strategy: Tidy or Change","description":"Strategy for Many More Much Smaller Steps. Kent Beck's Tidy First? framing: structural and behavioral changes go in separate changesets. Choose the order based on context — tidy first to make the change easy, or make the change then tidy. The constraint: never both in the same changeset. In a structural PR, any behavioral diff is a bug. In a behavioral PR, any structural diff is noise. Mixing them makes review unreliable. Lineage: Kent Beck, Tidy First?","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-12T11:00:52Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:00:52Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"When writing this strategy, reference: Grow Don't Break (bs-uzp) for when flags serve as the migration mechanism for breaking changes. Service Boundary Communication (bs-v9e) for where flags live in a multi-service architecture. Tidy or Change (bs-p73) for how flags enable shipping structural changes separately from behavioral ones."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-jgv","title":"Create strategy: Flag Your Changes","description":"Strategy primarily for Many More Much Smaller Steps, also serves Don't Fly Blind and No Shared Fate. Ship behind feature flags to merge domain design early and limit blast radius (50/50 motivation). Key points: flags default to OFF — code is wired but inert until explicitly enabled. Flag lifecycle: rollout flags (temporary, plan cleanup before shipping) vs targeting flags (long-lived, first-class config for different deploy targets or customer segments). Walking skeletons behind flags let the team build on new domain design immediately. Rollback becomes 'flip a flag' with granular targeting (specific users, 1% → 5% → 100%). Note FEAL interaction: a flag defaulting to OFF is an explicit design choice, not a silent path — no FEAL violation. Cross-cutting: serves MMMSS (early integration), DFB (canary-style rollout gives signal), NSF (isolates blast radius).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-12T11:00:59Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:00:59Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"When writing this guideline, reference: Domain-First Packaging (bs-u06) for how Go package restructuring works in practice. Public API Surface Testing (bs-2ay) for the _test package convention that enforces public API testing."}
old_value: {"id":"bs-8bi","title":"Create Go guideline for Intentionally Public","description":"Go-specific guideline implementing the Intentionally Public strategy (under Structure Is Intent). Key Go-specific content: default to _test packages to force testing through the public API. When _test testing becomes painful, use internal/ packages rather than exporting implementation details. Smurf naming avoidance is handled separately under BYN (bs-n5q). The guideline should make step 4 (design signal → restructure) concrete for Go: what does restructuring look like in Go specifically?","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ba@bjorn.now","created_at":"2026-03-11T22:22:55Z","created_by":"Björn Andersson","updated_at":"2026-03-11T22:22:55Z"}
